meta.subject: physics
meta.topic_name: Motion of Blocks Connected by String

intro.kicker: Topic 18 of 22 • Chapter: Newton’s Laws of Motion • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.topicName: Motion of Blocks Connected by String
intro.description: Analyses two or three blocks on a horizontal surface connected by a massless string, driven by an external force F. Unlike blocks in contact (where the connecting force is a contact/compression force), here the connection is a tension T in the string. Common acceleration a = F/(m₁+m₂), tension T = m₁ F/(m₁+m₂) for the block farthest from F. For three-block systems, two independent tension values T₁ and T₂ can be derived for each string segment.

expectedQuestions.description: String tension in connected horizontal blocks is among the most frequently tested NEET connected-body problems; appears in roughly 1–2 questions per paper.

timeRequired.description: Two-block derivation — 20 min; three-block multi-string case — 25 min; direction-switched problems — 10 min.

difficulty.description: Acceleration calculation is trivial once formulated correctly. The difficulty lies in identifying which string tension to compute and isolating the correct sub-system block(s) for the FBD.

nriUsaGap.description: US AP Physics covers tension in two-body systems well, but three-string segment problems and the direction-reversal variation are more commonly tested in NEET.

weightage.sectionTitle: NEET Weightage — Blocks Connected by String (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: Tension in the string between two horizontal blocks is the single most tested Newton’s Law numerical.
Three-block string tension (T₁ vs T₂) appears when three blocks must be differentiated in one MCQ.

strategySteps.title: Whole-System Acceleration First
strategySteps.description: Compute a = F/(m₁+m₂+...+mn) using the full system treated as a single mass. String tensions are internal and cancel at this stage.

strategySteps.title: Isolate the Sub-Block for T
strategySteps.description: For the string tension between blocks A and B, draw the FBD of the block farthest from F. Net force on it = T = mᵣ̵ × a, where mᵣ̵ = mass(es) on the far side of the string.

strategySteps.title: Distinguish from Contact Force
strategySteps.description: Strings can only pull (tension). Contact forces can only push (compression). In string problems, the connecting force is tension T; in contact problems, it is a normal/contact force f. The formulas are structurally identical but the physical mechanism differs.

cards.title: String-Connected Blocks — Complete Notes
cards.description: Two-block and three-block string tension derivations with all directional variations, FBD diagrams, and fully solved NEET MCQs on tension differentiation.

cards.title: Two Blocks Connected by String
cards.coreRevision: F applied on m₂, with m₁ connected by string. System acceleration a = F/(m₁+m₂). String tension (which must accelerate only m₁ on the far side): T = m₁ F / (m₁+m₂).
points: T is less than F; the string only pulls the front block
cards.example: m₁ = 2 kg, m₂ = 3 kg, F = 25 N: a = 5 m/s²; T = 2×5 = 10 N.

cards.title: Three Blocks — T₁ and T₂
cards.coreRevision: Three blocks m₁ (front), m₂ (middle), m₃ (rear) with strings T₁ (between 1–2) and T₂ (between 2–3). F on m₃ (rear end). Common a = F/(m₁+m₂+m₃). T₂ = (m₁+m₂) F / total mass. T₁ = m₁ F / total.
points: T₂ > T₁ always (since T₂ must pull more mass)
cards.example: m₁ = 1, m₂ = 2, m₃ = 3 kg, F = 12 N: a = 2 m/s²; T₁ = 2 N; T₂ = 6 N.

cards.title: Force Applied on Front Block (Reversed)
cards.coreRevision: If F acts on the front block m₁ (instead of the rear), the acceleration remains the same but the string tension formula now contains the rear block mass(es).
cards.example: F = 12 N on m₁ = 1 kg, m₂ = 2 kg, m₃ = 3 kg: T₁ = 10 N; T₂ = 6 N.

curriculum.hintText: AP Physics C covers two-body tension well. NEET extends to three strings and tests whether a student can correctly identify which sub-system to isolate.

gaps.title: Three-String Tension Identification
gaps.description: US physics problems rarely present three blocks and ask for a specific one of two tensions. NEET does this regularly, requiring systematic isolation of the correct sub-group.
bullets: Draw the full system diagram first; label every string with its own tension symbol.
For each string, isolate the sub-group on one side and write Newton’s 2nd Law for only that sub-group.

gaps.title: Tension vs Contact Force Conceptual Clarity
gaps.description: Strings can only be under tension (pull). A rope that would go into compression simply goes slack. Contact forces (adjacent blocks touching) can only push. NEET tests this conceptual distinction explicitly.
bullets: If the string goes slack (T = 0), the blocks move independently.
Contact force = 0 means blocks separate. Both limiting cases appear in NEET MCQs.

questions.question: Two blocks of mass 3 kg and 5 kg are connected by a string and placed on a smooth horizontal surface. A force of 16 N acts on the 5 kg block. What is the tension in the string?
questions.explanation: a = 16/8 = 2 m/s². Tension = 3 × 2 = 6 N (acts on the 3 kg block farthest from F).

questions.question: Three blocks A (2 kg), B (4 kg), C (6 kg) are connected by strings in series on a frictionless surface. A force of 36 N is applied on C. What is the tension in the string between A and B?
questions.explanation: a = 36/12 = 3 m/s². String between A and B pulls only A: T = 2 × 3 = 6 N.

questions.question: Blocks m₁ = 1 kg, m₂ = 2 kg, m₃ = 3 kg connected by strings. F = 18 N on m₁ (front). Find the tension in the string between m₂ and m₃.
If both tension and contact situations give the same formula structure for the two-block case, why are they different concepts?
options: Tension is a pulling force; contact is a pushing force
Tension can be zero or negative; contact cannot
Tension acts along the block; contact acts perpendicular
They are physically identical and the distinction does not matter
questions.answer: Tension is a pulling force; contact is a pushing force

items.title: Identify system and compute a first
items.description: Treat all connected blocks as one inertial system: a = F / (sum of all masses).

items.title: Draw separate FBDs for each block
items.description: Label tension arrows pointing inward on each block from the string. Tension pulls the block.

items.title: Isolate sub-system on far side for T
items.description: String tension T₁ = (mass of sub-system behind T₁) × a.

items.title: Distinguish tension direction
items.description: A string can only pull. If your calculation gives negative T, re-check the direction of assumed motion.

items.question: Why does the tension formula look the same as the contact force formula?
items.answer: Both result from Newton’s 2nd Law applied to a sub-system: force = sub-system mass × common acceleration. The structural form is identical. The physical difference is that contact forces are compressive (push), while string tensions are tensile (pull). This matters when the problem involves ropes that could go slack.

items.question: Can I have different tensions in the same string at different points?
items.answer: For a massless string (as assumed here), no. The tension is uniform throughout any section of a massless string. For a massive string, tension varies with position. That case is covered separately in the next topic (Motion of Massive String).

items.question: What happens if a string breaks?
items.answer: If the string carrying T₂ breaks, the sub-group of blocks it was pulling separates and continues with the acceleration it had at that instant. The remaining blocks re-equilibrate at a new (higher) acceleration since less total mass is being driven.

grounding_summary: Page 198 presents the complete table for Motion of Blocks Connected by Massless String with two-block and three-block configurations in both forward and reversed force directions.
Formulas confirmed from OCR: T = m₁F/(m₁+m₂) for the single front-block case; T₁ = m₁F/total, T₂ = (m₁+m₂)F/total for three-block cases.
Research data confirms string-connected horizontal blocks as the highest-frequency tension problem in NEET physics.
grounding_excerpt: Motion of Blocks Connected by Mass Less String

sources.title: NCERT Physics Part I — Chapter 5: Laws of Motion
sources.why_used: Canonical treatment of Newton’s Second Law for connected systems; confirms tension as internal force when treating entire system.

sources.title: Newton’s Laws NEET 2025 Practice Questions (NEETprep)
sources.why_used: Confirms string-connected horizontal block tension appears in PYQ banks and current NEET question sets; provides context for difficulty level and question format.

review.notes: Content grounded in page-198 OCR which contains the full massless-string table for two and three block systems. All four directional configurations covered. Formulas exact per OCR table.
